metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Studio Arts 2D 12 is the capstone senior visual arts course for students in the 2D pathway. Students synthesize their technical mastery, critical thinking, and personal artistic voice in the creation of a substantial, cohesive body of work — a capstone portfolio demonstrating sustained inquiry, artistic development, and cultural awareness. First Peoples visual art traditions are studied and engaged with depth and respect. This course prepares students for post-secondary fine arts, design, and art education programs.
metadata.general_description: The capstone senior 2D visual arts course synthesizing technical mastery, personal artistic voice, studio inquiry, art critical analysis, and professional portfolio development — preparing students for post-secondary fine arts, design, and related programs.

big_ideas.statement: Artistic mastery integrates technical skill, personal vision, and cultural responsibility.
big_ideas.elaboration: At the capstone level, visual artistry demands not just technical fluency but a coherent personal vision and a thoughtful understanding of the artist's responsibility to the communities, traditions, and contexts from which they draw.

big_ideas.statement: A cohesive body of work reflects sustained inquiry and a developed artistic voice.
big_ideas.elaboration: Professional visual art practice is not just about individual works — it is about developing a sustained conversation with ideas, materials, and meaning over time. A cohesive portfolio demonstrates the depth of that inquiry.

big_ideas.statement: Art both reflects and challenges the culture and context in which it is created.
big_ideas.elaboration: At this level, students are positioned to understand and contribute to the broader conversations that art participates in — social, political, environmental, and cultural.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate the highest level of technical mastery in 2D media; develop a cohesive body of work reflecting a personal artistic vision; take sophisticated and purposeful creative risks.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate the highest level of technical mastery across 2D media.
I can develop a cohesive, sustained body of work that reflects a clear and personal artistic vision.
I can take sophisticated, purposeful creative risks that advance my artistic inquiry.

competencies.raw: Critically analyze artworks with sophistication; lead critique and feedback processes; articulate the relationship between art, culture, and society.
i_can_statements: I can critically analyze artworks — including my own — with sophisticated vocabulary and theoretical depth.
I can lead critique processes that contribute meaningfully to the artistic development of peers.
I can articulate with nuance how visual art reflects, challenges, and shapes culture and society.

competencies.raw: Present a professional-quality portfolio; write a substantial artist statement; exhibit work in formal gallery or public contexts.
i_can_statements: I can present a professional-quality portfolio that demonstrates technical mastery, personal voice, and sustained artistic inquiry.
I can write a substantial, clearly articulated artist statement that positions my work in artistic and cultural context.
I can exhibit my work in formal gallery or public contexts, handling all aspects of installation and presentation.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate ethical and cultural responsibility in art-making; engage with First Peoples visual art as part of living cultural practice; prepare for post-secondary visual arts pathways.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate consistent ethical and cultural responsibility in my art-making practice.
I can engage with First Peoples visual art as a living, evolving cultural practice with depth and respect.
I can prepare thoughtfully for post-secondary fine arts auditions, portfolio reviews, and program applications.

areas.name: Capstone Studio Practice
areas.raw: Creating a substantial, cohesive body of 2D studio work.
topics: Sustained studio inquiry: developing a concept, series, or theme over the full semester
Technical mastery across chosen 2D media
Iterative process: research, thumbnail sketches, maquettes, and revisions
Critique participation: giving and receiving in-depth critical feedback
Documenting process: studio journal, artist diary, and work-in-progress photography

areas.name: Art Criticism and Theory
areas.raw: Advanced critical analysis and art theory.
topics: Critical frameworks: formalism, semiotics, feminist theory, postcolonialism, and ecocriticism
Writing about art: formal analysis, contextual analysis, and interpretive essays
Comparing artworks across cultures and time periods
Reading primary sources: artist manifestos, interviews, and critical texts
Contemporary art criticism: engaging with current discourse about visual art

areas.name: First Peoples Visual Art and Contemporary Indigenous Practice
areas.raw: First Peoples 2D art traditions and contemporary Indigenous artists.
topics: Northwest Coast formline design in depth: principles, forms, and regional styles
Contemporary Indigenous 2D artists: Daphne Odjig, Lawrence Paul Yuxweluptun, and others
Indigenous art and decolonization: reclaiming visual sovereignty
Protocols and ethics: engaging respectfully with Indigenous artistic traditions
Land, identity, and visual storytelling in First Peoples art

areas.name: Portfolio Development and Post-Secondary Preparation
areas.raw: Professional portfolio, exhibition, and post-secondary preparation.
topics: Portfolio curation: selecting, organizing, and presenting a cohesive body of work
Digital portfolio: photographing artwork professionally and building an online presence
Gallery exhibition: installation, labelling, artist statement, and opening event
Post-secondary portfolio review preparation: understanding program requirements
Career pathways: fine arts, design, illustration, arts education, and arts administration
